speaker: interviewee
text: --'96. I left you in the hotel with the Russians.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. There was a couple dozen people. And we had tried to escape Poland. And the KBG, or in [INAUDIBLE] there, I believe both, at that time, had been working the same-- same branch. They put us overnight in a hotel. I woke up early in the morning, when-- when it was still dark, I escape. By here someone told me the story, the and a couple of the KBG took-- took all the people from the hotel on a convoy. They ship out them to the Russia. So I was lucky.

speaker: interviewer
text: [LAUGHS] That's how you got back to Munich?

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, and we-- you said when you came to America, that you had another kind of job as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I used to work in the-- in the steel mill, melting the iron. Somewhere, I don't know where. Then I work in the-- I been working in the [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: At one point I work in a small [INAUDIBLE] near Linden, up in New York, in Tarrytown, the factory, Chevrolet. You know, familiar to you? Then I been working in-- in the airport, in the Kennedy Airport. What did you do at Kennedy airport?

speaker: interviewee
text: National Airline, we clean the airplanes. At that time, the National Airline we were-- travel to Cuba. Then I've been working in post office. They didn't want to let me go. I quit.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you started doing this kind of work?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because in our city, what kind of work I can take? I can-- I'll work also in-- [INAUDIBLE] Cup. You know, they made that place from paper cups to [INAUDIBLE] the cups in Jersey somewhere. You name it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you keep on working? You're still working. You still have energy. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: I tell ya, boy, I knew everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: Incredible. Now, in terms of what you suffered in the camps, and-- do you have any pain-- anything in your body that reminds you of those years?

speaker: interviewee
text: Do you know, I can say to you every bone in my body was broken. But in life, my spirit is powerful.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you just don't think about it?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Absolutely. I told my lady, I have-- I drove my lady there-- and there, where-- which I work part time.

speaker: interviewee
text: She's sitting at the counter. And she said, you know what, Walter? I don't think so many people are like you.

speaker: interviewer
text: I don't know what that means. You have to tell me one important thing though. You got married. When-- where did you get married?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to-- to France. I got married.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you meet your wife?

speaker: interviewee
text: By the-- Patty-- Patty knew it better than me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me the story. The-- my wife, she'd been in school to learn-- to learn how to-- to be what you call it? Like, a housekeeper, be good cook, everything. So I meet one lady-- one-- like a monastery. The monastery school. Some rich Frenchman give that building for the-- for the-- Convent, for the nuns?

speaker: interviewee
text: Convent, yeah. I meet a nun, which she was a principal. And she recommended her to me, my wife.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was a matchmaker that was working--

speaker: interviewer
text: --for you? And what is your wife's full name?

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was her maiden name?

speaker: interviewer
text: And you have two beautiful daughters.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Caroline. And we are in Caroline's apartment doing--

speaker: interviewer
text: We're filming today. I wanted to ask you, before we close the tape, why you felt that you wanted to do this tape? What do you think?

speaker: interviewee
text: There have to be more witness as possible about the Holocaust. Because I hear one man Canada. I believe he's German. And they were-- a couple months ago, there was even a youth on the radio. He said there was no such things. There was Holocaust in a-- in a-- in a camp. I was so angry. Furious. And--

speaker: interviewee
text: And we have to prove it. And there will be proof of it. I mean, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Polish-Jewish, friend Jewish, all Jewish suffer and pay-- pay life in the Holocaust, for no reason.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you for sharing your story with us. It was very well told.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- and that document was identification-- verification I was in a camp. And it was also signed by five-- five witnesses. We-- which don't know each other. That's why. And that was in Munich, I believe, 19--

speaker: interviewer
text: And this is a picture of you?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I was in the army.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is when you were working for the American Army?

speaker: interviewee
text: Uh-huh. We transported SS to American depot, for the depot, SS prisoner, from where I am, from Munich to different places. I was a little bit tough for them.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the place where we're securing the American convoy, the train with the American food for the-- for their army. And it was a bag of oranges, apple, eggs, you name it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were on the right?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I'm on the right, standing.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the two other men were friends?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, they were friends of mine. There was cigarette in the car too.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they was not-- not-- not in a camp. They was sent to civil-- civil work-- work in Germany, civilian.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the-- from green uniform, they blended American uniform on the dark blue, like a navy blue. And late-- lately, when the [INAUDIBLE] company, she depart from Munich to [INAUDIBLE], the German, they call us Polish SS just because of that uniform. We would have been tough for Germany.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right side, that's me. I am still wearing, here, American uniform. I don't want to [INAUDIBLE] and a friend of mine with the dark one, blue. I refused. [LAUGHTER] Then after I-- I will be another camp, I don't want to be in the black uniform. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: That picture is mine. In the barracks, I'm watching-- I was-- as guard, a security and the gate. Not to let them in the barrack, without pass. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: This is for the US Army?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the US Coast Guard pass. Which-- which this is the reason why-- why I work for the American ships. With-- without that pass, I could not go-- work on the American ships.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that's how you did your traveling?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was about 20 years ago. And gee, I forgot.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're talking in-- in the-- in the '40s, when you came here? Or the '50s?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was on the-- on the ship in the bunk hall. I believe we went to Saigon, transport the camera [INAUDIBLE] tanks to the Saigon during the Indochina, when there was war.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this is when you're still working for the Marines?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the picture of when I went on a visit, Auschwitz. This [INAUDIBLE] I believe the outside, in between the fence. And like a long, long passage, [INAUDIBLE]. I believe maybe 20-- 20 years ago.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this is three of us, Patricia, me, and Caroline. That's in a-- a Polish Council, on Madison and 37th Street in New York.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you. OK, this is the-- I receive it from the Polish government, a cross from the Auschwitz, signed by President Walesa.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this is why you were honored at the Polish Embassy?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, in Polish Embassy in-- in the Madison and 37th Street, in New York City.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when did this take place?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, that was signed by President Walesa in 1995. OK, this 'Legitymacja,' it's [INAUDIBLE] from-- from President Walesa. All together, with that cross from the Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this, again, was in 1995, in New York?

speaker: interviewer
text: At the Polish Embassy, thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, that was the original from Polish archive room from Auschwitz. Those document are very important. That was during the SS administration in Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, those document also, I believe, from a-- from hospital, or from Birkenau. That's affiliated of the-- of the Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this is at the time you were in Birkenau?

speaker: interviewee
text: This document is from Polish Museum, from Auschwitz. That's a [INAUDIBLE], a [INAUDIBLE] document.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the comment which I wrote to the Polish Red Cross to-- in Cracow, to ask for the three pictures from the Auschwitz, which my three pictures disappear. Someone got-- stole it, I believe, from the-- by the SS, or Dr. Mengele or the other--

speaker: interviewer
text: During the time you were in hospital?

speaker: interviewer
text: To the states that they do not have them?

speaker: interviewee
text: They don't have any no, no.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, this-- this is also document from the Auschwitz, from the-- museum-- museum. And they also say when I was born and what place. They are arresting 15-8-1940. My number in Auschwitz was 2254. That's-- and that's the important document.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, this is all general document from-- from the Polish Museum, Auschwitz. Those are my name, day-- day of birth, and then number 22-- --54. and the date arresting, 12-8-1940. Oh yeah, I was arresting by the calling SiPo. I don't know how to say it, like-- like a gestapo, SiPo.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the comment-- copy from the-- I believe from Gusen I, Gusen II, with the number, 44805. And then 24-1-1944. That's from Birkenau. Not Birkenau, Gusen I--

speaker: interviewer
text: These are the sub camps of Mauthausen.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you. OK, that document, from person who work for the Polish Museum, one of the lady, many, many books she sent me from Auschwitz. And beautiful letter.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she was a woman who you communicated with to get all your documents?

speaker: interviewer
text: And we don't know if she's still there at Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's why this is the reason why I went last year, to Poland. I tried to contact her.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mieczyslaw Kolodziejek, Patricia Kolodziejek, Caroline Kolodziejek, and Philip--

speaker: interviewer
text: So we have the family here with us?

speaker: interviewer
text: Would anybody like to say anything?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I-- I would, actually. Just that this is a great opportunity to record just another piece of history. And hopefully that it enables the world never to forget something that may repeat itself down the road. And to me, my father is my hero. And that's what I'd like to say.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Thank you for being with us and letting us into your home. Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I would like to add, also--

speaker: interviewee
text: --that we hear that lots of Jewish people had perished. But it's not only them. And it's also lots of Poles and French, and even Germans. It should be-- we-- we should be talking about that too, not only one side. It hurts when I hear only, you know, about a certain group of people that perish and not others that suffered as well and perished. It hurts a lot.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, that's what we're here today.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you're with us. And we're with you. And thank you. Thank you for sharing your story.
